coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and chlorine co-doped carbon dots to enhance the efficiency of dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Ghadari, Rahim
Sabri, Alireza
coredata.dc:description: A novel and facile hydrothermal route is reported for preparing the phthalocyanine supported on nitrogen and chlorine doped carbon dots (phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs) with extraordinary stability in water. Two types of phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs are synthesized using green carbon, nitrogen, and chlorine sources. Using N/Cl-CDs to support phthalocyanines led to high stability of these phthalocyanines in water. The computational studies of the phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs suggest their application as sensitizing systems in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Following this, the usability of phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs systems in DSSCs was studied. The obtained data indicated that good photoconversion efficiencies were extracted from the devices containing phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs. The prepared phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs showed effective sensitizing ability; it confirmed that N/Cl-CDs can act as an effective co-sensitizer in phthalocyanine-sensitized solar cells (Pc-SSCs). Based on the computational results, adding the N/Cl-CDs is lowering the band gap. For example, in the case of NiPc, the band gap was reduced from 2.22 eV to the 0.014 eV. This phenomenon is causing to easier transfer of electron from valance band to conducting band. In addition, presence of CO2H moieties is causing the lower band gap. Computational studies are revealed that the Ni2+ and Zn2+ do not contribute in the isosurfaces of orbitals. In addition, it was found that the transfer of electron between dyes and CDs could be occurred. In another word, transfer of electron from CDs to the dye can be observed. Based on the partial density of states (PDOSs) results, the contribution of metal ions in orbitals in most of the cases are negligible; although in cases such as NiPc and NiTCPc the metal ion is showing some contributions in HOMO/LUMO orbitals. The results showed that N/Cl-CD is playing an important role on the properties of orbitals such as HOMO and LUMO.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), particle size distribution (b), and EDS analysis (c) of the NiPc@N/Cl-CDs.
TEM image (a), particle size distribution (b), and EDS analysis (c) of the ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs.
TEM image (a), particle size distribution (b), and EDS analysis (c) of the NiTCPc@N/Cl-CDs.
TEM image (a), particle size distribution (b), and EDS analysis (c) of the ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs.
J-V curves of the Pc-SSCs using the MPc@N/Cl-CDs-loaded TiO2 (M = H2, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn) as the photoanode.
J-V curves of the Pc-SSCs using the MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs-loaded TiO2 (M = H2, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn) as the photoanodes.
Structure of optimized ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs; a representative example of MPc-, MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs structures.
Isosurfaces of orbitals that have the most important role in the electron transition by applying light.

Selected orbitals for MPc- and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs structures that are playing important roles in the electronic transitions induced by light. The italic numbers are indicating the no. of orbitals. Other images are provided in Fig. S18.

PDOS analysis performed on selected structures. The red line is showing metal, the blue line is indicating the organic section of MPc and MTCPc (without metal), and pink line in the PDOS at the left column is showing the contribution from N/Cl-CDs section. In all of the cases, the black line is showing the Total DOS (TDOS). In some cases (for example ZnTCPc) the contribution of metal in PDOS very little and there is overlap between blue line (organic section of ZnTCPc) and black line; therefore the black line cannot be seen clearly. Other images are provided in Fig. S19.
The PDOS of N/Cl-CDs. The frag. 1: Cl, frag. 2: O, frag. 3: N, frag. 4: C, and frag. 5: H.
Characteristic values of the Pc-SSCs using the MPc@N/Cl-CDs-loaded TiO2 (M = H2, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn) as the photoanode.
Characteristic values of the Pc-SSCs using the MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs-loaded TiO2 (M = H2, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn) as the photoanodes.
Comparison of the results obtained from ZnTCPc-based DSSCs.
Selected absorptions of pristine MPc and MTCPc structures and MPc– and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs structures. The electron transitions that have major contribution on the each λ, are provided as well.
The energy gap between orbitals that have the most important role on the absorption of light. (Energy unit: eV).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and chlorine co-doped carbon dots to enhance the efficiency of dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 5166616471 Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 5166616471 Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and facile hydrothermal route is reported for preparing the phthalocyanine supported on nitrogen and chlorine doped carbon dots (phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs) with extraordinary stability in water. Two types of phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs are synthesized using green carbon, nitrogen, and chlorine sources. Using N/Cl-CDs to support phthalocyanines led to high stability of these phthalocyanines in water. The computational studies of the phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs suggest their application as sensitizing systems in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Following this, the usability of phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs systems in DSSCs was studied. The obtained data indicated that good photoconversion efficiencies were extracted from the devices containing phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs. The prepared phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs showed effective sensitizing ability; it confirmed that N/Cl-CDs can act as an effective co-sensitizer in phthalocyanine-sensitized solar cells (Pc-SSCs). Based on the computational results, adding the N/Cl-CDs is lowering the band gap. For example, in the case of NiPc, the band gap was reduced from 2.22 eV to the 0.014 eV. This phenomenon is causing to easier transfer of electron from valance band to conducting band. In addition, presence of CO2H moieties is causing the lower band gap. Computational studies are revealed that the Ni2+ and Zn2+ do not contribute in the isosurfaces of orbitals. In addition, it was found that the transfer of electron between dyes and CDs could be occurred. In another word, transfer of electron from CDs to the dye can be observed. Based on the partial density of states (PDOSs) results, the contribution of metal ions in orbitals in most of the cases are negligible; although in cases such as NiPc and NiTCPc the metal ion is showing some contributions in HOMO/LUMO orbitals. The results showed that N/Cl-CD is playing an important role on the properties of orbitals such as HOMO and LUMO.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Some Data are provided as SI.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decades, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) emerged becoming popular among the scientific community as low cost solar cells [1–3]. Aiming at easy fabrication, desirable efficiency, and low cost manufacturing make DSSCs potential systems that can contribute in a sustainable energy production. Improving the solar-to-electricity conversion efficiency is key part of the researches in DSSCs [4]. In DSSCs, to perform different functions such as charge transport, photon absorption, and charge separation different compounds and layers should be used. DSSCs consist of a working electrode, a counter electrode and an electrolyte filled between them. In common DSSCs, the working electrode is the photo-anode which consists of dye adsorbed semiconductor layer [5]. It is usual to use organometallic dyes to adsorb light. When the photo-anode places under illumination, photo-excited dye injects electrons into the conduction band of semiconductor nanoparticles. Dyes are part of the key elements in DSSCs because they are directly involved in light harvesting, photoelectron generation, and electron transfer [6]. One of the main issues in the best performing DSSCs is the use of expensive dyes based on ruthenium complexes. The relatively low molar extinction coefficients of ruthenium-based dyes and their high synthesis cost hampers the widespread use of these complexes in DSSCs. To overcome the mentioned drawbacks of ruthenium-based dyes, alternative organic dyes have been proposed, such as natural dyes, porphyrin, and phthalocyanine derivatives [7–9]. The alternative organic dyes have some advantages such as higher molar extinction coefficients and low synthesis cost compared to ruthenium-based dyes but they have disadvantage of narrow spectral bandwidths. The narrow spectral bandwidths of the organic dyes leads to reduced power conversion efficiencies. Therefore, the search for light-harvesting materials with broaden light absorption and/or stronger light absorptivity is in great demand to encourage a wider use of organic dyes in DSSCs. Recently, the efficiency of the solar cells has been improved by the co-sensitization of dye molecules by using quantum dots (QDs) [10]. Carbon nanomaterials are outstanding candidates for optoelectronic applications because of their excellent optical properties, high surface area, chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, high photostability, and superior water dispersibility [11]. Deep eutectic solvents (DESs), as a new class of ionic liquids, obtain by using hydrogen-bond acceptor and hydrogen-bond donor materials [12]. Recently, deep eutectic solvents have drawn increasing attention because of their minimal ecological footprint. Biodegradability, biocompatibility, renewability, and low toxicity of DES components make them attractive candidates in many applications [13,14]. The use of DESs as carbonaceous precursors was reported to prepare porous carbon-based materials [15]. DESs ionic liquids can be used as unique candidates for preparation of carbon-based nanomaterials because of their capability to template the structure of the resulting carbonaceous materials. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbonaceous nanomaterials that have attracted widely public attention. The CDs have the potential to be used as alternatives to metal-based quantum dots due to their low cytotoxicity. Also, it has been reported that CDs can be synthesized in the eco-friendly approaches by using environmentally friendly materials such as various plant materials, biocompatible ionic liquids, and natural DESs. One-step hydrothermal approach to fabricate nitrogen and chlorine co-doped carbon dots from choline chloride-urea DESs has been described which the prepared structures showed excellent photo-luminescent properties [16]. DESs-assisted preparation of nitrogen/chlorine-doped CDs has been reported for biological sensing and imaging [17].

Recently, it has been demonstrated that preparative routes for chemical synthesization can be applied by DESs as environmental friendly media [12,18]. Therefore, in the present work, the phthalocyanine supported on nitrogen and chlorine doped carbon dots (phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs) were synthesized with a novel and green hydrothermal route. The phthalocyanine dyes were synthesized in choline chloride and urea based DESs as reaction media. In continue, the prepared phthalocyanine dyes in DESs were placed in hydrothermal conditions to prepare dual-functional carbon dots i.e., nitrogen and chlorine doped carbon dots (N/Cl-CDs). It is remarkable that DESs can be used as both reaction media for the synthesis of phthalocyanine and carbonaceous, nitrogenous, and chlorine precursors for the preparation of nitrogen and chlorine doped carbon dots. The computational studies of the phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs suggest their application as sensitizing systems in DSSCs. In our previous study, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were used to improve the efficiency of the phthalocyanine-based DSSCs. The result of the mentioned study indicated that the overall efficiency of system was improved by using NCDs [19]. As a result, in continue of previous studies about using nanoparticles to improve the efficiency of DSSCs [19–21], in the present work the usability of N/Cl-CDs on the improvement of the phthalocyanine-based DSSCs is studied. The chemical and thermal stability of both of phthalocyanine derivatives and carbon dot systems can provide extraordinary photostability of the prepared multi-functional carbon dots in solar devices.

1,2,4-Benzenetricarboxylic acid (C9H6O6, ≥99.0 %), urea (CH4N2O, ≥99.0 %), phthalic anhydride (C8H4O3, ≥98.0 %), metal(II) sulfate, metal(II) chloride, ammonium heptamolybdate (H24Mo7N6O24, ≥99.3 %), ammonium sulfate (H8N2O4S, ≥99.5 %), choline chloride, citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥99.5 %), terpineol (C10H18O, ≥80.0 %), tetrapropyl orthotitanat (C12H28O4Ti, ≥ 98.0 %), and ethyl cellulose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck. All solvents (methanol, ethanol, acetone, acetic acid) were obtained from Aldrich or Merck and used without further purification. FTO substrates, Pt paste, and standard iodine-based electrolyte were obtained from SharifSolar (IRASOL). Surlyn was purchased from SUNLAB. Titanium oxide nanopowder (TiO2, anatase, 99 + %, 10–25 nm) was obtained from US Reasearch Nanomaterials, Inc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phthalocyanine@nitrogen and chlorine doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: A choline chloride-urea deep eutectic solvent (DES) is first prepared by the reported procedures [22–24] and used without further purification. 2.8 g of choline chloride and 2.4 g of urea (with a molar ratio of 1:2) were dried at 60 °C for 24 h in an oven. The dried choline chloride and urea were then mixed and stirred at ca. 80 °C in air until a uniform liquid eutectics mixture was obtained. The prepared clear liquid is the choline chloride-urea deep eutectic solvent.

To prepare metallophthalocyanines in DES, the previously reported method was used [25]. In brief, a mixture of phthalic anhydride or 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid (2.0 mmol), metal salts (0.5 mmol), and ammonium heptamolybdate (0.01 mmol) in the as-prepared choline chloride-urea DES was heated at 140 °C for 2 h. After completion of the reaction, the prepared mixture of metallophthalocyanine in DES was used without further purification.

The phthalocyanine@nitrogen and chlorine doped carbon dots were prepared by the method similar to a described report [16]. Briefly, 1.9 g of citric acid and 100 mL of deionized (DI) water were taken to mix in a beaker with the prepared mixture of metallophthalocyanine in DES under stirring until the homogeneous solution is observed. The prepared mixture was allowed to undergo further reaction in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, dark blue mixture was obtained. Then, the mixture of reaction was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min twice to remove the large particles and the supernatant was collected. The products were dialyzed for 12 h through a dialysis membrane. Freeze-drying method was used to obtain the solid products for further characterization. The prepared phthalocyanine@nitrogen and chlorine doped carbon dots were identified as MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs in which phthalic anhydride and 1,2,4-benzenetricarboxylic acid were used as starting materials, respectively. The synthesis procedure of phthalocyanine@nitrogen and chlorine doped carbon dots are shown in Scheme 1 .

The photo-anode as the working electrode was fabricated by described report [26]. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrates (15 Ω/m2) were sequentially cleaned with DI water, HCl (0.1 M), acetone, and ethanol in an ultra-sonication bath, each for 20 min. The clean FTO substrates were dried under a nitrogen gas flow before titanium isopropoxide treatment. The titanium isopropoxide (Ti(i-pro)4) treatment was conducted by immersing the clean FTO substrates into Ti(i-pro)4 (40 mM) solution in a beaker at 50 °C for 30 min to form a compact TiO2 layer. The Ti(i-pro)4 treatment was followed by rinsing the FTO substrate with DI water and methanol and drying. The photo–electrode pastes containing the transparent and reflector TiO2 pastes were obtained using TiO2 NPs with the size of 20 nm and 300 nm, respectively. The mentioned photo–electrode pastes were prepared by sonicating a mixture of TiO2 NPs, ethyl cellulose, terpineol, and acetic acid in ethanol. The transparent and reflector TiO2 pastes were then doctor bladed onto the Ti(i-pro)4-treated FTO substrates, respectively. The printing of the photo–electrode pastes was followed by gradual sintering steps at 125 °C (15 min), 325 °C (5 min), 375 °C (5 min), 450 °C (15 min), and 500 °C (30 min) in muffle furnace under airflow. The annealed mesoporous TiO2 layers were once again immersed in Ti(i-pro)4 solution (40 mM) at 50 °C for 30 min and then rinsed with DI water and methanol. The TiO2 layers were annealed at 500 °C for 30 min in air. The surface-treated TiO2 film was cooled to 80 °C before being immersing into a solution of dye. The prepared electrode was immersed in the aqueous solution of the phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs (4 mg/mL) for 24 h to allow for sufficient dye adsorption.

Platinum (Pt)-coated FTO glass was used for counter electrode. The counter electrode was prepared by printing the Pt paste on drilled–clean FTO glass by the doctor blade method. The preparation of counter electrode is followed by sintering of Pt paste at 460 °C for 15 min in a muffle furnace under an air atmosphere. The working electrode and counter electrode were sandwiched with thermoplastic spacer (Surlyn film) with an open area of 0.25 cm2. The Surlyn film was used as a spacer and sealant to sandwich the prepared electrodes together by heating at 120 °C during 90 s. The electrolyte based on iodine consisting of iodine, lithium iodide, 1,2-dimethyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide, and 4-tert-butylpyridine in acetonitrile was injected into the cell through the holes drilled on the counter electrode and then the holes were sealed with a piece of the Surlyn spacer. Schematic representation of DSSCs is shown in Scheme 2 .

FT–IR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer. The optical absorption characteristics of the prepared samples were evaluated using a Shimadzu UV-2100 spectrophotometer. Determination of metals were done using FAAS (Shimadzu AA-680 flame atomic absorption spectrometer) with Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn hollow cathode lamps at 240.7, 248.3, 279.5, 232.0, 213.9, and 324.7 nm, correspondingly. An acetylene/air flame was used. The inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) Varian Vista PRO Radial was used to determine the mentioned metals, as well. X-ray diffraction pattern of compounds was recorded by STOE STADI P equipment with a scintillation detector. Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1540 nm) was used as secondary monochromator. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observations were carried out on a scanning electron microscope (TESCAN Vega Model). All samples were sputtered with gold before observation. LEO 906 transmission electron microscope was used to further analyze the nanomaterials. 100 kV acceleration voltage was used in the instrument. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) method was applied to study the size of nanocompounds. To do that, Nanotrac Wave equipment was used. Raman spectra of the prepared carbon dots were recorded on a Bruker SENTERR (2009). Photovoltaic measurements were performed under illumination generated by an AM 1.5 solar simulator (Fan Daghigh Kosar, SIM2002) with a 300 W xenon lamp and an air-mass 1.5 global filter. The power of the simulated light was calibrated to 100 mW/cm2 by using a reference silicon solar cell. The J-V curves were obtained by applying an external bias to the cell and measuring the generated photocurrent with a Fan Daghigh Kosar model IV2000 digital source meter. The voltage step and delay time of the photocurrent were 15 mV and 1 s, respectively.

All of the structures were prepared using GaussView (Ver. 6) [27] and optimization of the structures were carried out using Gaussian 09 (Rev. A01) [28]. Optimization of the phthalocyanine structures have been done using B3LYP method. For atoms other than metal ions (including H, C, N, and O) 6–311G(d) bases set was used. LANL2DZ basis set was used for metals and to consider the relativistic effects on the heavy atoms, effective core potential (ECP) have used, as well. To overcome the problems related to converge of the metal–containing structures, quadratically convergent (QC) self–consistent field (SCF) procedure was used. In addition, all of the calculations have been performed using “verytight” keyword for SCF. Calculations have been carried out in gas phase. The time–dependent density–functional theory (TD–DFT) calculations have used, too. In the case of pure phthalocyanine compounds, the level of theory similar to the optimization step has been used. In the structures that are containing carbon dot (CDs), the ONIOM method was used. The CDs structure was considered at low level and PM6 method was used to consider this section. The phthalocyanine sections have been considered as high level and B3LYP method was used. The bases sets similar to the optimization step were considered in these studies. For pure phthalocyanine structures, number of states equal to 15 have been considered. In structures that are containing CDs, the number of states equal to 25 has been defined. SCF with QC and “verytight” keywords were used in this section as well.

The adsorption of incident photons is playing an important role on the efficiency of a solar cell. To obtain the maximum amount of efficiency, it is crucial to adsorb the photons especially in the visible and infrared regions. Using phthalocyanine dyes in DSSCs is very usual, because they can adsorb light in the visible range. By the way, it is essential to improve the photon-to-current process to reach the maximum efficiency of a solar cell. In the present work, N/Cl-CDs are used to improve the efficiency of Pc-SSCs. The N/Cl-CDs were prepared by using the choline chloride-urea deep eutectic solvents (DESs). Therefore, the stable in water phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs systems were synthesized with a novel and green hydrothermal route. Both of phthalocyanine dye and N/Cl-CDs co-sensitizer are prepared with the assistance of a choline chloride-urea deep eutectic solvent. The eco-friendly multi-functional carbon dots with co-sensitization features are highly desired to be used in DSSCs. The prepared dual-functional doped carbon dots are hydrophilic systems due to their oxygen-rich nature with hydroxyl and amine groups. The joint combination of the phthalocyanine dye and proper hydrophilic N/Cl-CDs co-sensitizer could afford eco-friendly DSSCs. Thereby the prepared sensitizing systems are paving the new way to fabricate low cost, nature-inspired, and eco-friendly solar devices.

FT-IR spectra of N/Cl-CDs is illustrated in Fig. S1 (electronic supplementary information; ESI). The broad band is observable in the range of 3101–3600 cm−1 that ascribed to the OH and NH stretching vibrations of hydroxyl and amine groups in the structure of N/Cl-CDs [17]. The absorption bands appeared at 2852 and 2922 cm−1 are assigning to the CH stretching vibrations of the CO-NH group and the absorptions at 1612 and 1583 cm−1 are due to its stretching vibrations. The stretching vibration mode of CN is observable at 1475 cm−1. The absorption band at 1055–1083 cm−1 is associated with the CO bending vibrations. From Fig. S1 (ESI), in addition to the mentioned characteristic absorption bands of N/Cl-CDs, the characteristic absorption bands of phthalocyanine structure are observable in the FT-IR spectrum of MPc@N/Cl-CDs and/or MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs.

To shed a light on the photophysical properties of the prepared systems, UV–Vis studies have been carried out. UV–vis adsorption spectra of the prepared nano-structures are presented in Fig. S2 (ESI). The prepared N/Cl-CDs, MPc@N/Cl-CDs, and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs are showing an absorption at around 350 nm which is associated with the n-π* transition of the carbon dots [29]. Phthalocyanine systems are well known to show the absorptions in the range of 300–400 and 600–800 nm. These absorption are named B- and Q-bands respectively [30]. In Fig. S2 (ESI) the UV–Vis spectra related to the structures MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs are provided. The comparison between these results is showing that the Q-band is altered in these two systems. In another point, the interaction between the phthalocyanine with N/Cl-CDs is altering the adsorption properties of phthalocyanine system. In the sun light, the region around the red/near-infrared area is well known to have the highest amount of photon; therefore, adsorption of light in this area by the dye is very important [8]. In MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, a strong adsorption in the mentioned region can be seen. As shown in Fig. S2 (ESI), MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs systems have both light-absorbing properties of phthalocyanine and carbon dots, which can be used well in solar cells. Because the solar cells are in direct radiation of sun light, increase of temperature will be seen in the system, therefore, the dye that is used in the system must have high amount of chemical and thermal stability. The phthalocyanine are well-known compounds from these two criteria and they can be used in DSSCs safely [31]. From other point of view, the phthalocyanine are electrochemically active and therefore they are very useful to be used in the solar cells [30]. A very important aspect of phthalocyanine system is that, it is possible to alter and adjust the redox behavior of them by adding and altering the metal ion in the system. For example, different metals such as Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, and Zn can be used in the central hole of the phthalocyanine. By adding different functional groups such as carboxylate functional group to the phthalocyanine systems, it is possible not only alter the electronic and redox properties of them, but also, the interactions between the dye and N/Cl-CDs can be changed. Based on the results of XRD analysis and by observing a broad single peak that is centered on the ca. 20–25° (2θ) the synthesis of graphitic structures can be confirmed. The XRD result related to the synthesis of nanostructures using citric acid in choline chloride-urea DES is provided in Fig. S3 (ESI) [15].

The Raman spectra of the prepared carbon dots are provided in Fig. S4 (ESI). From the Raman spectra, a D band at around 1332–1352 cm−1 is observable that is due to sp2 hybridization imperfection and disordered induce-scattering. The small size of the sp2 cluster and destruction of the conjugated structure by oxygen-containing functional groups lead to D-band detection. The prepared carbon dots show the G band at around 1563–1579 cm−1 related to the E2g mode of graphite and the vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [32].

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis is used to detect the size of the prepared MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs nanostructures. As shown in Fig. 1-4
and S5-S15 (ESI), the diameter of the prepared structure was estimated to be relatively wide size distribution of nanodots.

By using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) method the elemental composition of N/Cl-CDs, MPc@N/Cl-CDs, and also MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs structures have been determine in a qualitative method (Figs. 1-4 and S5-S15 (ESI)). Based on the results that has been provided in Fig. S5 related to the N/Cl-CDs, it is possible to confirm the presence of elements such as Cl, N, C, and O. This observation is confirming the formation of the required structure. The results of the EDS analysis, related to the MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs were completely consistent with expected elemental analysis of the mentioned nanostructures. The EDS results confirmed the presence of mentioned elements in all of the phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs. Also, in each especial structure the presence of the metal ions were confirmed by considering the result of EDS analysis.

The morphology of the prepared nanostructures were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Circular morphology and well dispersed nanostructures of carbon dots was observed in the TEM image of them (Figs. 1-4, S7, S11, S12). TEM image of the prepared NiPc@N/Cl-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 1(a). From the TEM image of NiPc@N/Cl-CDs, the spherical and well dispersed nanostructures are observable without agglomeration in the aqueous medium. From TEM image, the diameter of NiPc@N/Cl-CDs was well consistent with the size detected by using DLS as shown in Fig. 1(b).

TEM image of the ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs is provided in Fig. 2(a) which good dispersion is shown in the structure. Also, the ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs nanostructures have relatively circular shape. The result related to the diameter of the ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs is in agreement with the size obtained by DLS analysis (Fig. 2(b)).

In Fig. 3(a) the TEM image related to the structure NiTCPc@N/Cl-CDs is provided. In the mentioned figure it can be seen that, the structure is showing good dispersion and no agglomeration is seen in the structure; also, it can be seen that the prepared nanostructures have relatively circular shape. The diameter of the NiTCPc@N/Cl-CDs was in agreement with the size that has been reported by DLS analysis (Fig. 3(b)).

As shown in Fig. 4(a), the ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs is characterized by TEM observation achieving a good dispersion and a circular shape without agglomeration in the aqueous medium. The diameter of ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs was well consistent with the size detected by using DLS (Fig. 4(b)).

Since the aim of the present work is to prepare a Pc-SSCs working with a green and eco-friendly sensitizing system, the phthalocyanine (acting as a sensitizer) has been selected to offer good performance and good biocompatibility. Taking into account the requirements of co-sensitizers for efficient and nature-friendly solar cells, carbon dots are preferred since they exhibit low toxicity than conventional inorganic quantum dots. Carbon dots have attracted the consideration of many research groups because of their high optical absorption, their high chemical stability, and their low toxicity. Therefore, N/Cl-CDs based choline chloride-urea DES was selected to be synthesize by using green and biocompatible starting materials. In particular, the selected phthalocyanine sensitizer can be synthesize with the assistance of choline chloride-urea DES. Using water as the reaction media is a very important method to prepare the eco-friendly sensitizing system; because, the water is an environment friendly solvent and at the same time it is not expensive. It has some other properties such as being non-toxic and non-flammable. Overall, the water can be used to design a clean synthesis process. Due to the environmental advantages of using water instead of organic solvents, the synthesis of the sensitizing system was done in water. So, the phthalocyanine dyes were synthesized in choline chloride-urea DES. Citric acid was added to the synthesized phthalocyanine in DES and the prepared mixture hydrothermally treated to produce phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs (MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs). The high dispersity of the sensitizing systems is essential for their high efficiency because aggregation of the sensitizing systems influence on their performance. Both of phthalocyanine molecules and carbon dots have high tendency to aggregation. Therefore, the dispersity of the prepared sensitizing systems were studied in water (as both reaction media and the dye-uptake solvent) to evaluate their stability and dispersibility. Studying the dispersity of the nanostructures in the presence and absence of the phthalocyanine is showing that the phthalocyanine not only is not decreasing the dispersity of the system, but also it is increasing the observed dispersity in some extent (Fig. S16). Both of the prepared sensitizing system (MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs) showed good dispersity and stability in water in long times (six months since these materials were synthesized). N/Cl-CDs shows very good dispersity and stability in water, therefore, the prepared MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs are stable in water. Due to the hydrophilic functional groups of the N/Cl-CDs based choline chloride-urea DES, the prepared carbon dots have the hydrophilic nature. Use of aqua soluble N/Cl-CDs leads the hydrophilic properties of the phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs, which consequently the stability and dispersity of the phthalocyanine dyes sensitizing systems were caused in aqueous solution (Fig. S16).

The stability and dispersibility of the prepared CDs is evaluated through UV–Vis spectroscopic technique. The UV–Vis spectra of the prepared MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs after a certain period of time are indicating that both prepared systems showed good dispersity and stability in water. Fig. S17 presents the selected UV–Vis spectra of the prepared MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs after a certain period of time. The prepared MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs systems feature excellent aqueous dispersibility and stability, which is attributed to the abundant surface-covered hydrophilic functional groups of N/Cl-CDs [16,17,33]. The surface of N/Cl-CDs is highly rich with oxygen in the forms of carboxylic acid and hydroxyl. These functional groups can contribute in hydrogen bonding with water. Also, amine functional group can act in similar manner. In addition of O and N related functional groups, the Cl can contribute in the making hydrogen bond interactions, too. The high amount of hydrogen bonding interaction between the prepared CD with water as solvent can be regarded as the main reason for the observed stability of the suspension of the system. Use of N/Cl-CDs increased the hydrophilic properties of the MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, which consequently the stability and dispersity of the prepared sensitizing systems were improved in aqueous solution. In fact, by immobilization of the insoluble phthalocyanines on the water soluble carbon dots, the stability and dispersibility of the prepared systems is improved.

The photo-anode dyeing process takes place in a dye solution; and different solvents are used depending on the nature of dye and its solubility. Phthalocyanines have poor solubility in many organic solvents and water, so un-substituted phthalocyanines are completely soluble only in concentrated sulfuric acid. Therefore, it is very difficult to choose a suitable solvent for phthalocyanine dyes that can completely dissolve the dye. Since the photo-anode dyeing process usually takes a relatively long time, about 16 to 24 h, the stability of the dye used in the dyeing solvent is very important. In this study, insoluble and slightly soluble phthalocyanines were investigated. Using the proposed green hydrothermal route to prepare sensitizing systems with the ability to be stable in water, the prepared sensitizing system is stable in the entire process of photo-anode dyeing in green solvent.

There are several reasons for choosing DES in this work: First, it can be used as a green and biocompatible solvent for the synthesis of phthalocyanine. Second and most importantly, DES itself could be used as a carbon, nitrogen, and chlorine precursor for the nitrogen and chlorine co-doped carbon dots synthesis. But we were amazingly surprised in the end because the prepared systems were so stable in water that they are six months old, but they are still very stable in water. The insolubility of the un-substituted phthalocyanines and low solubility of some substituted phthalocyanines in water and many organic solvents are the most important limitations of the mentioned dyes to be used in DSSCs. By using this green hydrothermal route to prepare phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs, not only the limitations of solubility of phthalocyanines and their aggregation was overcame. But, both sensitizer (phthalocyanine dye) and co-sensitizer (N/Cl-CDs) were synthesized together in an easy and biocompatible way.

The phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs were prepared by using un-substituted and carboxyl-anchored phthalocyanine dyes. The MPc@N/Cl-CDs systems were prepared by using H2Pc, MnPc, FePc, CoPc, NiPc, CuPc, and ZnPc phthalocyanines and named H2Pc@N/Cl-CDs, MnPc@N/Cl-CDs, FePc@N/Cl-CDs, CoPc@N/Cl-CDs, NiPc@N/Cl-CDs, CuPc@N/Cl-CDs, and ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs, respectively. Also, the prepared MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs systems contain H2TCPc, MnTCPc, FeTCPc, CoTCPc, NiTCPc, CuTCPc, and ZnTCPc phthalocyanines named H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs, MnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, FeTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, CoTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, NiTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, CuTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, and ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, respectively. In continue, the prepared MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing systems were applied to sensitize the solar cells. The amounts of the manganese, iron, cobalt, nickel, copper, and zinc of the MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs were calculated using FAAS method. The MnPc, FePc, CoPc, NiPc, CuPc, and ZnPc contents in the MnPc@N/Cl-CDs, FePc@N/Cl-CDs, CoPc@N/Cl-CDs, NiPc@N/Cl-CDs, CuPc@N/Cl-CDs, and ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs were determined 5.03, 5.88, 6.50, 5.74, 6.30, and 4.68 wt%, respectively. The MnTCPc, FeTCPc, CoTCPc, NiTCPc, CuTCPc, and ZnTCPc contents in the MnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, FeTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, CoTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, NiTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, CuTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, and ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs were determined 6.38, 7.88, 9.06, 7.27, 8.59, and 6.24 wt%, respectively. To determine the amount of metals in the mentioned systems, the ICP-AES method was used. The results of this analysis were consistent with the results of FAAS method and both methods were reported a similar amount for the studied metal ions.

In order to investigate photovoltaic properties of the phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs, the solar cells were fabricated with TiO2-coated FTO glass as anode, platinum (Pt)-coated FTO glass as cathode, and the prepared phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs as a sensitizer. It should be mentioned that phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs was selected as sensitizing system because it possesses a simpler structure of dye conjugated with N/Cl-CDs as co-sensitizer. Also, phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs have relatively low-cost synthesis in comparison with the widely used Ru-based dyes as sensitizer and inorganic QDs as co-sensitizer.

Due to the unique properties of CDs such as low cytotoxicity, good hydrophilicity, excellent optical properties, outstanding conductivity, and so on, CDs were selected in the present study [34–37]. The optical and physicochemical properties of CDs can be affected by internal electronic structures [38]. By adding heteroatoms it is possible to alter the structure of carbon materials. It can cause to change and improve the electronic structure/conductivity and chemical states [39]. By adding nitrogen to the system it is possible to cause an induction of negative charge in comparison with pure carbon framework. The result of it will be improving the electron transfer process [40,41]. By adding chlorine atoms it is possible to introduce new energy levels. It will cause a tunable luminescence behavior and also polychromatic fluorescence [42]. Based on previous reports, by using two different atoms for doping, it is possible to control the intrinsic properties of carbon dots with more details [43]. Also, using different elements can cause to a change in the light absorption/emission properties of carbon dots. Therefore, in the present study, nitrogen and chlorine co-doped carbon nanodots were selected to improve the efficiency of the phthalocyanine-based DSSCs.

Current-voltage characteristics of the phthalocyanine-sensitized solar cells in the presence of the prepared phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs systems in simulated sunlight are shown in Figs. 5 and 6
and the cell parameters are summarized in Tables 1 and 2
.

Fig. 5 shows the J–V curves of Pc-SSCs using different MPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing systems, with the key parameters, including short-circuit photocurrent density (JSC), open-circuit voltage (VOC), fill factor (FF), and power conversion efficiency (PCE) summarized in Table 2.

For having an effective DSSC system, the dye must be able to adsorb the high amount of incident light and the dye should be able to inject the excited electron to the semiconductor layer. The second process must be faster than relaxation of the excited state to the ground state.

It was mentioned before that the phthalocyanines show two main bands at 300–400 nm (B-band) and 620–700 nm (Q-band); the extinction coefficients for the Q-band range is determined to be in the order of 105 M−1 cm−1 [44]. By altering the substituents, it is possible to extended or shift the mentioned absorption bands. Therefore, ZnPc and ZnTCPc were selected as base dyes to evaluate the efficiency of solar cells in the presence of N/Cl-CDs co-sensitizer. It can be seen from data related to ZnPc (Fig. 5 and Table 1) and ZnTCPc (Fig. 6 and Table 2) that the cell sensitized by ZnTCPc show higher JSC relative to ZnPc. The presence of the carboxylate moieties on the ZnTCPc is causing the chemisorption of the mentioned structure to the surface of TiO2. This can simplify the injection of the electron from the excited state of dye to the conducting layer. The interaction between the dye and titana moiety is facilitating the transfer of charge from the exited dye to the semiconductor layer. The overall result of this behavior is the increase of JSC in comparison with ZnPc-based solar cell. After that, N/Cl-CDs co-sensitizer was used to increase light harvesting and reduce recombination in the Pc-SSCs. Solar cell sensitized by ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs reached a PCE of 3.24 % which is extremely higher than that of N/Cl-CDs-free device (Fig. 5, Table 1). Also, ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs as sensitizing system was used to construct solar cell structure which an extremely high PCE of 3.76 % with a higher JSC and VOC than bare ZnTCPc-DSSC was obtained (Fig. 6, Table 2). The obtained data indicated that efficient charge injection from the prepared sensitizing systems to TiO2 can occur and that N/Cl-CDs can act as an effective co-sensitizer in Pc-SSCs. It suggested that the N/Cl-CDs is causing an increase in the light absorption area of solar cell and at the same time the mentioned nanostructure is facilitating the separation of hole-electron pairs; also causes to an increased charge extraction process [45]. Analyzing the factors contributing to the increase of PCE of ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs and ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs to N/Cl-CDs-free devices indicated that the most important factor is the increase in JSC. The observed improvement can be related to the N/Cl-CDs. This nanostructure enables more utilization of photons by the solar cells. Because, the JSC is related to the number of photons with energy higher than the band gap that are reaching the dye [46]. Another important factor contributing to the increase of PCE is the increase in VOC. It can be seen from Figs. 5 and 6 that the Pc-SSCs in the presence of ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs and ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs exhibit higher VOC relative to N/Cl-CDs-free devices, which should be benefitted from the lower lying EHOMO of phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs because it is well known that the VOC is related to the band gap of sensitizer [46]. The difference between the redox potential of the electrolyte and the Fermi level of the anode can be used to express the VOC. In the cases that the N/Cl-CDs are used as the co-sensitizer, the electron of the N/Cl-CDs can be added to the conduction band of the TiO2. This will cause to an increased electron density of the TiO2 that will lead to higher VOC values [47]. Another important reason for increasing the efficiency of ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs and ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs systems compared to N/Cl-CDs-free devices is that the dispersion of phthalocyanine molecules in the presence of N/Cl-CDs is extremely increased, which prevent them from aggregation.

The dependence of device performance on central metal in the phthalocyanine was investigated based on a device with the structure glass/FTO/TiO2/MPc@N/Cl-CDs/electrolyte/Pt/FTO/glass. The results for different metal in the core of phthalocyanine are presented in Fig. 5 and are summarized in Table 1. From the obtained results, the VOC values for all MPc@N/Cl-CDs were between 0.51 and 0.60 V, whereas the JSC and FF values showed remarkable differences depending on the metal in the core of phthalocyanine. It was found that when the H2Pc@N/Cl-CDs is used to sensitize the solar cell, the device show current leakage and low VOC and FF comparing other MPc@N/Cl-CDs which the core of the phthalocyanine is metallated. The lowest performance was observed for the H2Pc@N/Cl-CDs which the core of phthalocyanine is metal-free. From the obtained data in Table 1, it is indicated that metalation of the phthalocyanine core in the MPc@N/Cl-CDs systems improved the efficiency of Pc-SSCs. Although the performances show variation by metalation of the central cavity of phthalocyanine using manganese, iron, cobalt, nickel, copper, and zinc, it is clear that ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs gives the best device performance, and FePc@N/Cl-CDs and CuPc@N/Cl-CDs yield inferior device performance. By metalation using iron and copper, the JSC and VOC in FePc@N/Cl-CDs and CuPc@N/Cl-CDs increased compared to H2Pc@N/Cl-CDs which leads to increase of PCE. The solar cells with CoPc@N/Cl-CDs and MnPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing system exhibit better performance in comparison to FePc@N/Cl-CDs and CuPc@N/Cl-CDs. Device with a ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing system show the highest JSC and VOC between all MPc@N/Cl-CDs, while device of NiPc@N/Cl-CDs has the highest FF. The best device performance was observed using ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing system, achieving a VOC of 596 mV, JSC of 9.23 mAcm−2 and FF of 0.59, leading to an overall device efficiency of 3.24 %. Based on the obtained data, the ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing system produces the highest VOC and JSC among MPc@N/Cl-CDs systems, but this system achieved FF of 0.59 which lead to an overall device efficiency of 3.24 %. The FF value of a device can be calculated as the ratio of the maximum power (PMax) of the solar cell to the product of VOC and JSC. The presence of recombination phenomena and/or high amount of resistance in the device can cause to the reduced FF in the system [46].

The J-V characteristics for Pc-SSCs based on various MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing systems under AM 1.5 G irradiation with an irradiation intensity of 100 mW/cm2 are shown in Fig. 6. The photovoltaic parameters derived from the J−V curves containing JSC, VOC, FF, PCE are summarized in Table 2. The device based on ZnTCPc sensitizing system gives a PCE of 0.36 % with JSC = 1.99 mAcm−2, VOC = 396 mV, and FF = 0.46. Interestingly, when the ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs is employed as sensitizing system, the JSC, FF, and VOC are increased to 9.87 mAcm−2, 0.59, and 646 mV, yielding a PCE up to 3.76 %. The low device performance for the ZnTCPc sensitizing system is caused by small JSC, FF, and VOC. But, the JSC, FF, and VOC was increased after sensitizing by ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs. By adding the N/Cl-CDs to the device, an increase and improvement in the solar cell was observed. This observation can be attributed to the increased photocurrent density of device. Two reason can be considered for the mentioned improvement. It is known that the N/Cl-CDs can contribute in the absorption of incident light. This can be regarded as the first reason for the mentioned improvement in the performance of device. Also, it has been mentioned that the N/Cl-CDs can facilitate the transfer of electron to the TiO2-based anode. This can be considered as the second reason for the improvement of performance of solar cell [48]. Compared to ZnTCPc-DSSCs, less recombination in the solar cell based on ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs increase the device's FF [46]. The VOC was increased when the device was treated with ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, which could be ascribed to the band gap of the sensitizing system [46]. The dependence of the Pc-SSCs efficiency on the metal in the core of phthalocyanine was investigated using MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing systems. The Pc-SSCs device based on the H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs yielded JSC = 4.33 mAcm−2 and VOC = 570 mV corresponding to PCE = 1.70 %, whereas the Pc-SSCs using other MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs showed higher JSC, VOC, and PCE. Between all MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing systems, the lowest Pc-SSCs performance was observed for the H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs which the core of phthalocyanine is metal-free. By metalation of metal-free phthalocyanine using iron, the JSC increased from 4.33 to 5.81 mAcm−2 yielding a PCE up to 2.02 %. The JSC increase in Pc-SSCs based on FeTCPc@N/Cl-CDs relative to H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs has occurred, indicating that metalation of the phthalocyanine core in the H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs system could improve the efficiency of Pc-SSCs. But, the PCE increase in the FeTCPc@N/Cl-CDs system is small, which is due to the decrease in FF of the Pc-SSCs based on FeTCPc@N/Cl-CDs compared to H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs. By metalation using copper and cobalt, the JSC in CuTCPc@N/Cl-CDs and CoTCPc@N/Cl-CDs increase significantly compared to H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs, which leads to increase of PCE. MnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, NiTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, and ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitized solar cells give higher power conversion efficiencies than other MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs-sensitized solar cells, as they give higher photocurrents and open-circuit potentials. Therefore, the metalation of the core of phthalocyanine in H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs system using manganese, nickel, and zinc could significantly improve the efficiency of Pc-SSCs. In comparison to other MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, the solar cell with ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing system exhibit the best performance.

The photovoltaic behavior of N/Cl-CDs shows a comparative performance of the devices fabricated with MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs synthesized using green hydrothermal technique. The comparison of the photovoltaic performance of current study and the related studies reported in literature show the significant importance and core content of this work. Using the N/Cl-CDs in ZnTCPc-containing photoanode in a solar cell device resulted in significant enhancements of the JSC, VOC, and PCE, respectively, relative to N/Cl-CDs-free device. The acceptable PCE (3.76 %), JSC (9.87 mAcm−2), and VOC (646 mV) are reported for ZnTCPc-containing cell co-sensitized with N/Cl-CDs in the present study. Therefore, the prepared N/Cl-CDs is an eco-friendly multi-functional carbon dots with co-sensitization features which it is showed highly desired performance in DSSCs. The joint combination of the phthalocyanine dye and proper hydrophilic N/Cl-CDs co-sensitizer is afforded eco-friendly DSSCs. It can be concluded that because of the simple and green synthesis and desirable performance in DSSCs, the synthesized phthalocyanine@N/Cl-CDs can paving the new way to fabricate low cost, nature-inspired, and eco-friendly solar devices. In Table 3 , the results of the present study have been compared to previous reports of ZnTCPc-based DSSCs. The easy preparation methods is one of the advantages of the present report. Also, carbon dots can be prepared via non-toxic and biocompatible approach.

As mentioned above JSC is important factor contributing to the increase of PCE in the co-sensitized device. To better understand the effect of conjugation between phthalocyanines and N/Cl-CDs on the performance of DSSCs, computational studies was done. Also, the influences of the central metal and carboxyl-substituent of the phthalocyanine dye in the prepared systems was computationally studied. To do that, the examination of electronic structures of the frontier orbitals of the prepared systems and phthalocyanine molecules is done.

To perform the calculations and study the effect of the N/Cl-CDs on the optoelectronic behavior of the MPc and MTCPc, a series of calculations have been performed in the absence and presence of the N/Cl-CDs. To create the structure of N/Cl-CDs, nitrogen, oxygen, and chlorine atoms have been placed in different positions, randomly. Oxygen atoms were considered as hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and epoxy moieties. The nitrogen atoms were considered in the structures as pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic ones. The dimension of N/Cl-CDs was considered to be 25 × 25 Å2 and the MPc/MTCPc structure was positioned in the central section of the N/Cl-CDs. Two different kinds of phthalocyanines considered in the present study. In one batch MPc structures do not have any kind of substations and in another batch, four CO2H moieties were added to them (MTCPc). The MPc/MTCPc structures were positioned about 10 Å away from the surface of the N/Cl-CDs and the structure have been optimized with the level of theory that has been provided in the computation methods section. An optimized structure is provided in Fig. 7 . To understand the effect of the N/Cl-CDs on the optoelectronic behavior of the phthalocyanines, the calculations on the pristine MPc and MTCPc structures have been carried out, as well.

To understand the effect of the N/Cl-CDs on the behavior of MPc and MTCPc structures, TD–DFT calculations have been carried out, as well. The selected absorptions are provided in Table 4 . In addition, the orbitals that are playing role for each of the reported transitions are provided. In the case of the pure MPc, the HOMO–1, HOMO, LUMO, and LUMO+1 have the most contribution in the electron transitions. In the MPc@N/Cl-CDs, the “numbers” of the contributing orbitals (and not the name of orbitals) in selected absorptions are provided.

The details about the contribution of the orbitals in selected transitions in structure NiPc, NiTCPc, NiPc@N/Cl-CDs, NiTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, ZnPc, ZnTCPc, ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs, and ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs are provided herein as representative examples. From Table 4 it can be seen that in NiPc, NiTCPc, ZnPc, and ZnTCPc the most important orbitals in the electron transitions are HOMO, LUMO, and LUMO+1 ones. The similar behavior can be seen in the N/Cl-CDs containing structures. In these cases, the transitions between the orbitals that are energetically close to each other have the most important contribution in the absorption of sunlight. For example, in NiPc@N/Cl-CDs and ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs structures the transitions that are including 141 → 142, 138 → 139, 141 → 143, and 138 → 140 have the most important role. In two of these transitions, orbitals are next to each other and as a result have the least gap. In other two transitions, the orbitals are not exactly next to each other. However, considering the differences of energies between these orbitals (Table 5 ) is showing that the gap between these orbitals is small enough and the transition of electron can be promoted by sun light. The results in the Table 5 is revealing an important point. The point is that, in the presence of the N/Cl-CDs the energy gap is reduced in comparison with pristine MPc and MTCPc in a very considerable amount. This observation can be considered as one of the important reasons for better performance of solar cells by adding N/Cl-CDs. The smaller band gap is facilitating the transfer of electron from the valance band to the conduction band of the system and as a result, higher JSC is observed in the structures that have N/Cl-CDs.

Another interesting point is that, in pristine MPc and MTCPc structures, the presence of CO2H moieties is causing to a reduction in the band gap. This can facilitate the transfer of electron from to the conducting band, which will cause to better performance of the solar cell. It should be mentioned that, better coordination of the MTCPc to the Ti is another reason that should not be ignored in the higher efficiency of solar cells that are containing MTCPC instead of MPc. The coordination of the CO2H moiety to Ti can facilitate the transfer of excited electron from dye to the semiconductor layer. This can cause to reduced recombination of electron/hole. The reduce recombination process will cause to better and higher performance of solar cell.

To understand the role and contribution of orbitals in the electron transitions by applying light, the isosurfaces of orbitals are studied and the images of these orbitals are provided in Fig. 8 . It is clear that, the metal is not contributing directly in the orbitals except LUMO and LUMO+1 of the NiPc and NiTCPc; also, the contribution of metal ion in the isosurface of orbitals is negligible in comparison with other sections of structures. In addition, it can be seen that the isosurfaces in the structures that have CO2H moiety and without that moiety are very similar. It was previously mentioned that the structures with CO2H moiety have lower energy gap in comparison with structures without CO2H. It seems that, the mentioned moiety is acting in an indirect way to facilitate the transfer of electron instead of direct altering the HOMO and LUMO and other orbitals in the structure. The metal ions are acting in a very similar way. It was mentioned that the Ni2+ and Zn2+ ions are altering the energetic properties of orbitals instead of appearance of orbitals. In addition, from the provided isosurfaces it can be seen that some of orbitals are degenerate by considering the images of orbitals (for example LUMO and LUMO+1 of NiPc) and just orientation of isosurface is changed. Considering the energy of these mentioned orbitals revealed that they are degenerate from energetic point of view, as well.

In continue, the role of the presence of the N/Cl-CDs in the orbitals of MPc and MTCPc are studied. The orbitals that have contribution in the important electronic transition related to the NiPc@N/Cl-CDs, NiTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs, and ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs are provided in Fig. 9 , as representative examples. The band gap related to the most important electronic transitions for the selected structures are provided in Table 5. Considering the results provided in Fig. 9 and Table 5 shows that presence of the CDs is playing two different roles in solar cell. In one way, it lowers the HOMO–LUMO gap. For example, in the case of NiPc–based solar cells, the band gap calculated to be 2.22 eV while in the case of NiPc@ N/Cl-CDs, the bad gap is predicted to be 0.014 eV. It is clear that the presence of N/Cl-CDs has a considerable effect on the band gap, which is leading to a high amount of improvement in the efficiency of solar cells. The lower band gap is causing to ease of electron flew between orbitals. In addition, it needs radiation with lower energy to transfer electron from low-energy layer to high-energy one. In another word, lower band gap is causing to easer transfer of electron from valance band to conducting band; this observation means higher JSC in the solar cell. The reason for the mentioned behavior can be concluded from the images of orbitals that are provided in Fig. 9 and Tables 4 and 5. In the case of NiPc@N/Cl-CDs structure, the transition from orbital 141 to 142 and from orbital 141 to 143 are playing the most important role. From the results, provided in Fig. 9 it can be seen that orbital 142 is located on the NiPc and orbitals 141 and 143 are located on the N/Cl-CDs section. In addition, transitions from orbital 137 to 142 can be seen. This electron transfer is including transfer of electron from N/Cl-CDs to the NiPc. In other words, in NiPc@N/Cl-CDs, the electron transfer between two systems can be seen. By using orbitals of both systems, it is possible to fill the gap of HOMO and LUMO of one system with the orbitals from other system to create new energy levels. By putting new orbitals between previous orbitals, new energy levels can be created with lower band gap. As it was mentioned earlier, the N/Cl-CDs is playing double role. In one way, it alters HOMO–LUMO gap. In another way, it is contributing directly in the absorption of light. For example, transitions such as 141 → 143 and 137 → 143 can be seen for NiPc@N/Cl-CDs. By considering the orbitals 137, 141, and 143, it can be observed that, these orbitals are completely located on N/Cl-CDs. As another example, ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs will be explained with more details herein. From Table 5 it can be seen that the band gap for ZnPc and ZnPc@QD are 2.17 and 0.055 eV, respectively. As before, the mixed electron transition between the orbitals of ZnPc and N/Cl-CDs is obvious (131 → 140); also, transition inside of the CDs is playing a role (134 → 140 and 136 → 140). Similar behavior can be observed for other MPc/MTCPc@CDs–based structures, as well. In all of the cases that MPc and N/Cl-CDs are used, the pure contribution of N/Cl-CDs in electron transitions and also mixed transition between the MPc/MTCPc and N/Cl-CDs can be observed.

Density of states (DOSs) is a powerful tool to investigate the energy levels; therefore, by using DOSs it will be possible to study the transition of electron between energy levels. The partial DOS (PDOS) can be used to study the contribution of different fragments of a system in each energy level. Herein the PDOS of MPc, MTCPc, MPc@N/Cl-CDs, and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs (M = Ni2+ and Zn2+), are presented (Fig. 10 ). Based on the results of the isosurfaces of orbitals that has been mentioned before, the contribution of metal ions in the HOMO orbitals are negligible. That observation can be confirmed based on the PDOS results of MPc and MTCPc structures. In some cases such as NiPc and NiTCPc, the contribution of metal in the LUMO level can be seen. It should be emphasized that, such a conclusion can be made based on the images of orbital but it is very difficult to find such behavior by those images; in a contrary manner, the PDOS results are showing the mentioned observation very clear. In the cases that the structure is containing N/Cl-CDs, the contribution from the mentioned moiety in the HOMO and LUMO can be seen in PDOS results. Based on the PDOS results, the N/Cl-CDs has a major contribution in both HOMO and LUMO orbitals and other orbital next to these ones. This is indicating that N/Cl-CDs is playing an important role on the properties of orbitals such as HOMO and LUMO. As a result, it can alter properties of orbitals such as their energy to make them more accessible by electrons; therefore light can easily transfer an electron from lower energy orbitals such as HOMO to higher energy orbitals such as LUMO. This overall process will improve the performance of solar cell.

In Fig. 11 , the PDOS of just N/Cl-CDs moiety is presented. Because the mentioned moiety is including different atoms (Cl, N, O, C, and H) it will be useful to show the influence and contribution of each atom kind in the energy levels of orbitals. In this case, it will be possible to adjust the energetic properties of CDs by altering the kind and amount of atoms. The PDOS results is indicating an interesting point. In the HOMO orbital the O and N have the highest amount of contribution while in the LUMO level, the contribution of the Cl atoms is increased, in addition to the O and N. Based on the mentioned observation, altering the amount of Cl, will have more effects on the LUMO. The N has high contribution in both HOMO and LUMO orbitals and contribution in LUMO is higher. In a contrary manner, the contribution of O in the HOMO is a bit higher than LUMO and altering the O content will effect HOMO more than LUMO, although the difference is low.

The phthalocyanine supported on N/Cl-CDs with extraordinary stability in water by a novel and facile hydrothermal route is reported. Two types of phthalocyanines (MPc and MTCPc) are used and MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs are prepared using green carbon, nitrogen, and chlorine sources. N/Cl-CDs was used to support phthalocyanines that led to high stability of these phthalocyanines in water. The computational studies suggest the application of MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs as sensitizing systems in DSSCs. Following this, the usability of MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs systems in DSSCs was examined. The devices containing MPc@N/Cl-CDs and MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs showed effective sensitizing ability in Pc-SSCs which confirmed the usability of N/Cl-CDs as an effective co-sensitizer. Based on the obtained data related to the MPc@N/Cl-CDs containing devices, the ZnPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing system produces the highest VOC and JSC and gives the best device performance. NiPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitizing system was placed in second rank. The lowest performance was observed for the H2Pc@N/Cl-CDs among all MPc@N/Cl-CDs systems. Based on the results related to the MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs containing devices, the lowest Pc-SSCs performance was observed for the H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs which the core of phthalocyanine is metal-free. ZnTCPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitized solar cell gives higher power conversion efficiency than other MTCPc@N/Cl-CDs, as it gives higher photocurrent and open-circuit potential. NiTCPc@N/Cl-CDs sensitized solar cell was ranked second. From data, metalation of the phthalocyanine core in the H2Pc@N/Cl-CDs and H2TCPc@N/Cl-CDs systems could improve the efficiency of Pc-SSCs. Computational studies revealed that by adding N/Cl-CD the band gap will be reduced. For example, in the case of NiPc, the reduction in the band gap from 2.22 eV to the 0.014 eV was observed. This will cause to easier transfer of electron from valance band to conducting band. In addition, it was found that the presence of CO2H is causing the lower band gap. Computational studies are shown that the metals such as Ni2+ and Zn2+ do not contribute in the isosurfaces of orbitals. In addition, it was found that the transfer of electron between dyes and CDs could be occurred. In another word, electron can be transferred from CDs to the dye. Based on the partial density of states (PDOSs) results, in most of the cases the contribution of metal ion in orbitals is negligible. It should be mentioned that in cases such as NiPc and NiTCPc the metal ion is showing some contributions in HOMO/LUMO orbitals. In contrast with metal ions, the results revealed that N/Cl-CD is playing an important role on the properties of orbitals such as HOMO and LUMO. All in all, low cost and easy to synthesis Pc dyes were used instead of expensive dyes such as N719 in the present study. To overcome the low efficiency of the Pcs as sensitizer, N/Cl-CDs were used. The process for the synthesis of the N/Cl-CDs is simple, too. By using these two low cost and readily available compounds, it was possible to obtain a solar cell with highly improved efficiency. By considering the synthesis/purification cost and ease of process, it is concluded that the introduced dye/QDs are valuable compounds to be considered in the DSSCs.
